evals.prompt: Purchases: 200 units at 50, then 300 at 60, then 100 at 70. We sold 450 units. Give me COGS and ending inventory under FIFO and periodic weighted average.
evals.expected_output: Total cost 35,000 for 600 units. FIFO: COGS = 200x50 + 250x60 = 25,000; ending = 50x60 + 100x70 = 10,000. Weighted avg 58.333: COGS = 26,250; ending = 8,750. Both proved to total 35,000.
assertions: FIFO COGS of 25,000 and ending inventory of 10,000
Weighted average COGS of 26,250 and ending of 8,750
Proves COGS + ending inventory = total cost under both methods
Uses code for the computation

evals.prompt: Our plant budgeted 600M fixed overhead at normal capacity of 50,000 units but only produced 35,000 this year due to FX-related input shortages. The plant manager wants to spread all 600M over the 35,000 units. Is that right?
evals.expected_output: No. Absorb at the normal-capacity rate of 12,000/unit (420M into inventory/COGS via units); the 180M unabsorbed overhead is expensed immediately. Inflating unit costs to 17,143 overstates inventory.
assertions: Rejects spreading total overhead over actual low production
Computes the normal rate of 12,000 per unit
Expenses 180M of unabsorbed overhead in the period
Cites the IAS 2 normal capacity principle

evals.prompt: An inventory line: 1,000 units at cost 800 each. Current selling price 850, but completion costs are 30/unit and selling costs 60/unit. Test for write-down and book the entry if needed.
evals.expected_output: NRV = 850 - 30 - 60 = 760 < cost 800 -> write down 40/unit = 40,000. Dr Inventory write-down (COGS) 40,000 / Cr Inventory allowance 40,000. Note IFRS reversal if NRV recovers.
assertions: Computes NRV of 760 deducting both completion and selling costs
Writes down 40,000 total
Books a balanced entry
Mentions IFRS reversal possibility up to original cost

evals.prompt: From the attached purchase/sale ledger (evals/files/purchases_sales.csv), compute COGS and ending inventory for the 150-unit sale under FIFO, weighted average, and periodic LIFO. State which gives the lowest taxable income in this rising-price environment.
evals.expected_output: FIFO COGS $1,600 / EI $2,000; WAC $1,800; LIFO $2,000. LIFO minimizes taxable income when prices rise.
assertions: FIFO COGS $1,600 and ending inventory $2,000
Weighted-average COGS $1,800
LIFO COGS $2,000
States LIFO gives lowest taxable income with rising prices (and notes IFRS prohibits LIFO)
Computes with code

evals.prompt: [adversarial] Compute FIFO COGS for the sale in the attached ledger (evals/files/oversold_ledger.csv).
evals.expected_output: Sale of 180 exceeds the 150 units purchased - impossible. Must flag the data error (missing purchases, opening balance, or wrong sale qty) and not fabricate a cost for the 30-unit shortfall.
assertions: Detects that units sold (180) exceed units available (150)
Does not invent a cost for the missing 30 units
Suggests likely causes (missing opening inventory or purchase records)
Verifies with code
